Joint feedback and adjustment cycles: Teams met after sessions to review what worked and what didn't, and co-adjust the next session, improving flow and adaptation over time.
Facilitator flexibility/adaptive delivery: Bloom facilitators who loosened the script, shared the spotlight, and co-facilitated with UDS staff enabled smoother integration.
UDS staff flexibility/initiative: UDS educators/nutritionists/pedagogues adapted their own planned activities to complement Bloom content.
Clear connectors between segments: Intentional transitions or shared activities that bridged Bloom segments with UDS components reduced 'fracture' and made the session feel whole.
Inclusion of children in the session: Designing activities where children are protagonists aligned with family modality norms and increased caregiver participation/attendance.
Caregiver-only blocks + second caregiver/childcare: For dense topics, temporarily separating children (or having a second caregiver) allowed caregivers to focus and reduced stress.
Age-based adaptations (0–2 years): Tailoring activities to infants and toddlers made content viable within UDS's younger age groups.
'Bloom time' anchor: Dedicating 15 minutes with the child provided a simple, memorable thread that integrated well with existing routines.
Rapport and psychological safety: Warmth and trust-building led to emotional openness and greater engagement.
Time flexibility within 2 hours: Allowing elastic time for transitions/snacks and blending didactic content with play/activity improved adaptation.
Use of existing UDS characterization: Drawing on knowledge of family profiles/interests aligned better with ongoing casework.
Low-burden journal use (at home): Positioning the journal primarily as a home tool maintained session flow while reinforcing transfer.
Child-friendly space setup: A thoughtful space configuration matched UDS practice and signaled a distinct, welcoming environment.
Family communication channels: Short WhatsApp videos, reminders, and testimonials fit existing outreach practices.
Tangible take-home resources: Books, audio/podcasts, or similar resources reinforced home practice and were valued.
Links to local services: Connecting practices to other community spaces extended relevance beyond the UDS setting.
